summary:
The Dzungar Khanate (c. 1634–1758 AD) was the last great nomadic empire of Central Asia — a western Mongolian (Oirat) state centered in the Dzungaria basin (modern Xinjiang) that challenged [[event:2337|Qing China]], Russia, and the various Kazakh and Tibetan states for dominance of Inner Asia, nearly succeeding in creating a rival empire to the Qing before being destroyed with systematic thoroughness in what is now regarded as one of the 18th century's most devastating genocides. The Dzungar khans — particularly Galdan (r. 1671–1697), Tsewang Rabtan (r. 1697–1727), and Galdan Tseren (r. 1727–1745) — proved formidable adversaries: they controlled the major trade routes of Central Asia, conquered Tibet (1717), and defeated Qing armies multiple times, forcing emperors Kangxi and Yongzheng to invest enormous resources in Central Asian campaigning.

The Dzungar state's end was sudden and total. When succession conflicts divided the khanate in the 1750s, the [[event:2463|Qianlong Emperor]] seized the opportunity for a final campaign: Qing armies invaded Dzungaria in 1755–1757, and Qianlong ordered the extermination of the Dzungar population — contemporary Qing reports describe the systematic killing of adult males and enslavement of women and children. Estimates of Dzungar deaths range from 480,000 to 800,000 — roughly 80% of the Dzungar population. The territory was repopulated with Han Chinese, Uyghur, Kazakh, and other settlers, becoming 'Xinjiang' ('New Territory'). The genocide eliminated the last nomadic threat to Qing security and completed the [[event:3299|Ten Great Campaigns]] that defined Qianlong's reign, but it also eliminated an entire people and a cultural tradition that had been central to Central Asian history for generations.
